[[commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] [[commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q710302]] Wikidata name: Matsunoo Taisha [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:04, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{infobox religious building | name = Matsunoo-taisha
松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[:en:Oyamakui no Kami|Ō-yamagui-no-kami]]
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, [[:en:Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|Nishikyō-ku]], [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]]
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located at the far western end of [[:en:Shijō Street|Shijō Street]], approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the [[:en:Arashiyama|Arashiyama]] district of [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from [[:en:Nara (city)|Nara]] to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and [[:en:Miso|Miso]] companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''[[:en:Hatsumōde|Hatsumōde]]''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, [[:en:Emperor Murakami|Emperor Murakami]] ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer [[:en:Mirei Shigemori|Mirei Shigemori]] was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of [[:en:Mount Penglai|Horai]] in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *[[:en:List of Shinto shrines|List of Shinto shrines]] *[[:en:Twenty-Two Shrines|Twenty-Two Shrines]] *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:

松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ō-yamagui-no-kami}}
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|WD=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|WD=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ō-yamagui-no-kami}}
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|WD=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|WD=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]]

Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

[[co:commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] [[co:commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] [[sn:commons:Category:Matsunoo-taisha]][[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]][[en:Matsunoo Taisha]][[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]][[it:Santuario Matsunoo]][[ja:松尾大社]][[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]][[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]][[zh:松尾大社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{infobox religious building | name = Matsunoo-taisha
松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ō-yamagui-no-kami}}
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|WD=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|WD=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:pages]]

[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|WD=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street|1=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|WD=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso|1=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori|1=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai|1=Mount Penglai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:pages]]

松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|WD=Q10935105|lt=Ō-yamagui-no-kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|WD=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street|1=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|WD=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|WD=Q235169|lt=Miso|1=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|WD=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|WD=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori|1=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai|1=Mount Penglai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|WD=Q798391|lt=Horai|1=Mount Penglai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]

松尾大社 | image = Matsuo Taisha honden2.jpg | caption = The ''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', at Matsunoo-taisha | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|01|N|135|41|07|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Oyamakui no Kami|en|Oyamakui no Kami|qid=Q10935105|lt=Ō-yamagui-no-kami|1=Oyamakui no Kami}}
[[:en:Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto|Nakatsu-shima-hime-no-Mikoto]]
[[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] | founded_by = | established = 701 | date_destroyed = | location = 3 Arashiyamamiya-chō, {{ill|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1154588|lt=Nishikyō-ku|1=Nishikyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}
[[:en:〒|〒]] 616-0024 | website = {{URL|http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/}} | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Taisha'''|松尾大社|extra=''Matsunoo Taisha/ Matsuo Taisha''}}, formerly {{Nihongo|'''Matsunoo Jinja'''|松尾神社}}, is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located at the far western end of {{ill|Shijō Street|en|Shijō Street|qid=Q7496525|lt=Shijō Street|1=Shijō Street}}, approximately 1.3 kilometers south of the {{ill|Arashiyama|en|Arashiyama|qid=Q2859566|lt=Arashiyama|1=Arashiyama}} district of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. It is home to a [[:en:Spring (hydrosphere)|spring]] at the base of the mountain, Arashiyama, that is believed to be blessed. It is said that during the move of the capital from {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}} to Kyoto, a noble saw a turtle bathing under the spring's waterfall and created a shrine there. It is one of the oldest shrines in the Kyoto area, its founding extending back to 700 CE. The restorative properties of the spring bring many local [[sake]] and {{ill|Miso|en|Miso|qid=Q235169|lt=Miso|1=Miso}} companies to the shrine for prayers that their product will be blessed. The shrine also serves a ''kinpaku miki'' (gold leaf filled blessed sake) during ''{{ill|Hatsumōde|en|Hatsumōde|qid=Q1032381|lt=Hatsumōde|1=Hatsumōde}}''. ==History== The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74–75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian ''[[kami]]'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Matsunoo Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116–117. From 1871 through 1946, Matsunoo-taisha was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. == Shofuen Gardens == After a new set of buildings was finished in 1973, modernist garden designer {{ill|Mirei Shigemori|en|Mirei Shigemori|qid=Q2749325|lt=Mirei Shigemori|1=Mirei Shigemori}} was brought in to design new garden areas at Matsuo Taisha. Shigemori designed three new garden areas, which were completed in 1975: * Kyokosui no Niwa 曲水の庭 (Garden of the Winding Stream) with a clear flowing stream in the style of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} * Jōko no Niwa 上古の庭 (Prehistoric Garden), a setting of large stones that references the ancient Mt. Matsuo shrine stone behind the shrine buildings * Horai no Niwa 蓬莱の庭 (Garden of Horai), a garden referencing the land of {{ill|Mount Penglai|en|Mount Penglai|qid=Q798391|lt=Horai|1=Mount Penglai}} in Chinese and Japanese myth The gardens at Matsuo Taisha were Shigemori's last project; his son, Kanto, supervised the final work after Shigemori's death on March 12, 1975.{{Cite book |last=Tschumi |first=Christian |title=Mirei Shigemori: Modernizing the Japanese Garden |publisher=Stone Bridge Press |year=2005 |edition=1st |pages=108–115}} ==images== File:Matsuo Taisha front gate.jpg|Front gate File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan01s3.jpg|Horai Garden of Shofuen File:Shofuen Matsuo-taisha Kyoto Japan07s3.jpg|Kyokusui Garden of Shofuen File:Tsukuyomi shrine Kyoto.jpg|Shrine to [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto|Tsukuyomi]] ==See also== *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|399449}} * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} ==External links== *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/en/ Official Site] {{in lang|en}} *[http://www.matsunoo.or.jp/ Official Site] {{in lang|ja}} {{Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ [[en:Matsunoo Taisha]] {{Commons category|Matsunoo-taisha}} [[de:Matsunoo-Schrein]] [[fr:Matsunoo-taisha]] [[it:Santuario Matsunoo]] [[ja:松尾大社]] [[my:မဆွတ်နိုအိုတိုင်းရှ]] [[ru:Мацуноо-тайся]] [[zh:松尾大社]] {{wikidata link|Q710302}} [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Buildings and structures completed in the 8th century]] [[Category:Gardens in Kyoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Junior Third Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fifth Rank]] [[Category:Junior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Second Rank]] [[Category:Senior Fourth Rank]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]]
